name: Proto-Levantine Arabic
notes: Proto-Levantine Arabic (شامي) — abstract ancestor node for Syrian, Lebanese, Jordanian, and Palestinian Arabic. DEFINING SEDENTARY FEATURES: ق qaf → /ʔ/ glottal stop in the prestige urban koines (Damascus, Beirut, Jerusalem, Amman) — with /ɡ/ in rural/Bedouin and Hawran/southern Jordan speech, and /k/ in central rural Palestinian; ج jim → /ʒ/ (voiced postalveolar fricative); ث/ذ/ظ interdentals VARIABLE — urban koines merge θ→/t/ (~/s/ in learned words), ð→/d/ (~/z/), ðˤ→/dˤ/ (~/zˤ/), while rural, Druze, and Bedouin speakers conserve the interdentals. ض → /dˤ/ (Classical lateral lost; ظ and ض both merge to /dˤ/ in urban speech). Imāla raises /aː/ → [eː] near front consonants, strongest in Lebanese. Source: Cowell (1964) A Reference Grammar of Syrian Arabic; Levantine Arabic phonology (Wikipedia); Holes (2004).

sources.author: Cowell, M.W.
sources.title: A Reference Grammar of Syrian Arabic
sources.notes: Standard reference grammar of Damascus Arabic; describes qaf→ʔ, jim→ʒ, interdental merger.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
